summary:
Aristotle (384–322 BC) was the most systematically comprehensive thinker of the ancient world — a philosopher, scientist, and educator whose surviving works cover logic, metaphysics, ethics, politics, rhetoric, poetics, biology, physics, astronomy, psychology, and more. A student of Plato at the Academy for twenty years, he founded his own school, the Lyceum, in Athens (335 BC), and tutored the young Alexander the Great (343–340 BC) — giving him a direct connection to the man who would transform the world.

Aristotle's intellectual range was unmatched: his Organon established formal logic as a discipline; his Nicomachean Ethics defined virtue ethics and the concept of eudaimonia (flourishing) that remains central to moral philosophy; his Politics analyzed constitutions and articulated the vision of humans as political animals; his biological works — the first systematic comparative zoology — described hundreds of animal species through direct observation and dissection. His influence on medieval Islamic philosophy (through translations by Al-Kindi, Al-Farabi, and Avicenna) and then on medieval European scholasticism (through Thomas Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotle with Christian theology) made him 'The Philosopher' — a figure whose authority in university curricula was so dominant that the Scientific Revolution partly defined itself against him. Despite supersession in specific sciences, his logical and ethical frameworks remain foundational.
